content.problemStatement: Once upon a time, there was a Kingdom of Seven Roads. Besides a fancy name, it actually had exactly 7 straight roads. Its residents wanted to keep track of the distances they traveled so they placed milestones along some roads. The roads slowly deteriorated and disappeared but some milestones remained. Archeologists documented remaining milestones and want to reconstruct the kingdom, starting with its main road. Help them by finding the maximum number of collinear milestones.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N – the number of documented milestones. The next N lines each contain two integers Xi and Yi separated by a space, the coordinates of a milestone.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum number of milestones that lie on the same straight line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case the points (0,0),(1,0),(2,0) are collinear, giving a maximum of 3. In the second test case only two points exist, so the answer is 2.
All four points lie on the line y = 2x, therefore the maximum collinear count is 4.
No three points are on the same line, so the best we can do is any pair, giving 2.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of slope. For each pair of milestones, we calculate the slope of the line passing through them. We use a hashmap to store the frequency of each slope. The maximum frequency of slope plus one (for the anchor point) gives us the maximum number of collinear milestones.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) per test case, O(T * N^2) total. The precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the hashmap used to store the frequency of each slope.

code.Python3:
import sys,math

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        pts = [(int(next(it)), int(next(it))) for __ in range(N)]
        best = 1 if N>0 else 0
        for i in range(N):
            slopes = {}
            xi, yi = pts[i]
            for j in range(N):
                if i==j: continue
                xj, yj = pts[j]
                dx = xj - xi
                dy = yj - yi
                g = math.gcd(dx, dy)
                dx //= g
                dy //= g
                if dx < 0:
                    dx = -dx
                    dy = -dy
                elif dx == 0 and dy < 0:
                    dy = -dy
                key = (dx, dy)
                cnt = slopes.get(key, 0) + 1
                slopes[key] = cnt
                if cnt + 1 > best:
                    best = cnt + 1
        out_lines.append(str(best))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
